---
title: "Lecture 2: History of Web & Internet, Protocols Governing the Web"
subtitle: "{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}"
author: "{{< meta instructor.name >}}"
date: "January 27, 2026"
code-annotations: below
---


## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

<div class="custom-title">
[Lecture 2]{.baby-blue-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle">
[History of Web & Internet, Protocols Governing the Web]{.white-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle3">
Week 1 | Unit I: Web Page Designing<br>
{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}<br>
{{< meta instructor.name >}}, {{< meta instructor.title >}}
</div>

---

## {data-menu-title="Learning Outcomes"}

[Learning Outcomes]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
After this lecture, students will be able to:

- explain the evolution of the internet and web
- identify key internet protocols and technologies
- describe how the web works behind the scenes
:::

::: {.notes}
Start with the importance of understanding the history to appreciate the current state of web technology.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Prerequisites"}

[Prerequisites]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Basic computer knowledge
- Familiarity with internet and web browsing
- Understanding of URLs and browser usage
- Basic awareness of client and server terms
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Syllabus Mapping"}

[Syllabus Mapping]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Syllabus mapping:

- History of the web and internet
- Key protocols and their roles
- How the web works end-to-end

Prerequisites used:

- Basic network concepts
- Understanding of URLs and browsers
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Agenda"}

[Agenda]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Internet history timeline
- Web evolution phases
- Protocols and request-response model
- DNS and client-server workflow
- Standards and governance of the web
- Summary and exam-focused preparation
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Introduction"}

[Introduction and Motivation]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- The modern web did not appear overnight; it evolved across decades of networking innovation.
- Understanding this evolution helps students connect protocols with practical web behavior.
- Historical milestones explain why standards, interoperability, and security matter today.
- This lecture builds conceptual clarity for upcoming HTML, CSS, and full request lifecycle topics.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Think About It"}

[Think About It - Interactive Questions]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Before diving deeper, reflect on these:

- Why does a domain name work even when we do not know the IP address?
- What would happen if DNS did not exist?
- Why does HTTPS matter more today than in the early web?
- Which part of the web flow is handled by your browser, and which by the server?
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Internet History"}

[History of the Internet]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{mermaid}
timeline
        title Internet and Web Milestones
        1969 : ARPANET launched
        1983 : TCP/IP standardized
        1989 : World Wide Web proposed
        1991 : First web page published
        1993 : Mosaic browser popularized access
```

::: {.body-text-m}
- The internet infrastructure evolved first, followed by web standards and browsers.
- Each milestone improved interoperability, accessibility, and global adoption.
:::

::: {.notes}
Explain that ARPANET was funded by DARPA and was designed to survive a nuclear attack by not having a central point of failure.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Web Evolution"}

[Evolution of the Web]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Web 1.0** (1991-2004)]{.teal-text}

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Static HTML pages
- Read-only content
- Limited user interaction
- Server-side focused
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Web 2.0** (2004-present)]{.teal-text}

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Dynamic content
- User-generated content
- Social media & collaboration
- Rich interactive experiences
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Web 1.0 was all about publishing information. Web 2.0 introduced social features and the ability for users to interact and share.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Internet Protocols"}

[Key Internet Protocols]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
::: {.fragment}
[**HTTP/HTTPS**]{.teal-text}: HyperText Transfer Protocol (secure)
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**TCP/IP**]{.teal-text}: Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**DNS**]{.teal-text}: Domain Name System (translates domain names to IP addresses)
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**FTP**]{.teal-text}: File Transfer Protocol
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**SMTP**]{.teal-text}: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="HTTP Request-Response"}

[How HTTP Works]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
[**HTTP Request**]{.teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- Method: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
- URL of resource
- Headers with metadata
- Optional body with data
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[**HTTP Response**]{.teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- Status code: 200, 404, 500, etc.
- Headers with info
- Body with content
- Cache directives
:::
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .body-text-s .center-text .topbr}
Both encrypted with HTTPS for security
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="How Web Works"}

[How the Web Works]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
1. User types URL in browser
2. DNS resolves domain name to IP address
3. Browser sends HTTP request to server
4. Server processes request and database queries
5. Server sends HTTP response (HTML, CSS, JS)
6. Browser renders the page on-screen
7. Browser loads additional resources (images, scripts)
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the fundamental workflow. Every interaction on the web follows this basic pattern.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Client-Server Model"}

[Client-Server Architecture]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Client** (Browser)]{.teal-text}

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Sends HTTP requests
- Renders HTML/CSS
- Executes JavaScript
- Stores cookies & cache
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[**Server**]{.teal-text}

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Listens for requests
- Processes business logic
- Queries databases
- Sends responses
:::
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="DNS Resolution"}

[Domain Name System (DNS)]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```
User enters: www.example.com
        ?
Browser queries DNS resolver
        ?
Resolver asks Root nameserver
        ?
Root responds with TLD nameserver
        ?
Resolver queries TLD nameserver
        ?
TLD responds with Authoritative nameserver
        ?
Resolver gets IP address
        ?
Browser connects to 93.184.216.34
```

::: {.fragment .body-text-m .center-text .topbr}
[This usually takes milliseconds!]{.magenta-text}
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Web Technologies Timeline"}

[Web Technologies Timeline]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
::: {.fragment}
**1991**: HTML - Created by Tim Berners-Lee
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1996**: CSS - Added styling capabilities
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1995**: JavaScript - Made web interactive
:::
::: {.fragment}
**1999**: PHP 3 - Server-side scripting
:::
::: {.fragment}
**2006**: jQuery - Simplified JavaScript
:::
::: {.fragment}
**2010**: AngularJS - Web applications framework
:::
::: {.fragment}
**2013**: React - Component-based UI
:::
::: {.fragment}
**2014**: Vue.js - Progressive framework
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Key Milestones"}

[Internet Growth Milestones]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.fragment}
[**2000s**]{.slide-title3 .teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- E-commerce boom
- Cloud computing
- Mobile web
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.fragment}
[**2010s**]{.slide-title3 .teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- Smartphones dominant
- Real-time apps
- Machine learning
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.fragment}
[**2020s**]{.slide-title3 .teal-text}

::: {.body-text-m}
- AI integration
- Web3/Blockchain
- Edge computing
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Open Standards"}

[Web is Built on Open Standards]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
::: {.fragment}
[**W3C** (World Wide Web Consortium)]{.teal-text}

Standards and guidelines for web technologies
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**IETF** (Internet Engineering Task Force)]{.teal-text}

Internet protocols like HTTP, TCP/IP
:::
::: {.fragment}
[**WHATWG** (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group)]{.teal-text}

Living standards for HTML
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .body-text-m .center-text .topbr}
[? This openness enabled global web growth]{.magenta-text}
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Live Demo"}

[Live Demo: DNS + HTTP Request Journey]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Interactive classroom walkthrough to connect lecture concepts with observable browser behavior:

?? **[Open Interactive Demo: Web Request Journey](../demos/lecture-02-demo.html){target="_blank"}**

What this demo shows:

- URL input and DNS resolution steps (visualized)
- HTTP request and response with status code examples
- secure vs non-secure protocol comparison (HTTP vs HTTPS)
- where client actions end and server actions begin

Use this during class while discussing protocol flow and troubleshooting logic.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Resources"}

[Resources & References]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- [Internet Society - History](https://www.internetsociety.org/internet/history-internet/)
- [MDN: How the Web Works](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/Getting_started_with_the_web/How_the_Web_works)
- [W3C History of the Web](https://www.w3.org/standards/history/)
- [Course content: Week 1](/content/week-01.qmd)
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Debug"}

[Structured Debug Checklist for Web Connections]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Investigation order when a website won't load:

1. verify URL is correctly typed and accessible
2. check browser network tab for HTTP status codes
3. inspect DNS resolution (nslookup, ping)
4. verify server is responding (check status pages)
5. check browser console for JavaScript errors
6. validate SSL/HTTPS certificate (if HTTPS issue)
7. test from different network to isolate issue

This approach separates client vs server vs network issues.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Summary"}

[Summary]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
After this lecture, you should understand:

- Internet history from ARPANET to modern web
- How HTTP and HTTPS power web communication
- The role of DNS in translating domain names
- Client-server architecture fundamentals
- Standards organizations maintaining web openness
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Exam Questions"}

[Exam Preparation Questions: Short]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- What is ARPANET?
- Define HTTP and HTTPS.
- What is DNS?
- State one difference between Web 1.0 and Web 2.0.
- What is the role of TCP/IP?
- What is a client in web architecture?
- What is a server in web architecture?
- What does a browser do after receiving HTML?
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Long Questions"}

[Exam Preparation Questions: Long]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Explain the evolution of the internet from ARPANET to modern web.
- Explain HTTP request-response cycle with a suitable flow.
- Differentiate HTTP and HTTPS with practical examples.
- Explain DNS resolution process in detail.
- Discuss web standards bodies and their role in interoperability.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Practice"}

[Practice Task]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- use `nslookup` or `dig` to resolve a domain name
- inspect HTTP requests in browser DevTools
- compare one HTTP page and one HTTPS page
- capture status code and headers for both
- write a short comparison summary
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Checklist"}

[Checklist]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Can you:

- explain internet history milestones?
- differentiate HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, and TCP/IP?
- describe DNS lookup and request-response flow?
- answer short and long exam questions confidently?
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Next Lecture"}

[Next Lecture]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Topic: HTML Introduction and Basic Structure
- Preparation required: revise tags, attributes, and document structure basics
:::

---

## {#end-slide data-menu-title="End Slide" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center">
<div class="custom-subtitle">
[History of Web & Internet, Protocols Governing the Web]{.white-text}
</div>
<div class="custom-subtitle3">
Next: Lecture 3 - HTML Basics & Structure
</div>
</div>
